items.title: INTERVIEW- Hadji Sow : « Je ne fais pas de politique, mais j’ai chanté le président Mamadi Doumbouya parce que j’apprécie sa gouvernance »
items.description: Deux ans après avoir achevé un album de 13 titres en soussou, maninka et pular, Hadji Sow, qui marche dans les pas de son défunt frère Seydou Sow, poursuit son parcours artistique avec détermination. Dans un entretien accordé à notre rédaction, la chanteuse revient sur ses débuts, l’influence de son frère, son morceau dédié au président Mamadi Doumbouya, sa vision de la musique guinéenne et ses ambitions. Interview… Mediaguinee : Depuis quand chantez-vous ? Cette passion est-elle liée à votre frère Seydou Sow ? Hadji Sow : Les enfants de ma mère, qui est albinos, aiment chanter depuis leur plus jeune âge. Nous ne sommes pas issus d’une famille de griots, mais le chant fait partie de notre quotidien. Que Dieu ait pitié de l’âme de mon frère Seydou Sow. Il souhaitait que je chante. C’est pourquoi il y a très peu de différences entre nous sur le plan musical. Lui était un homme, moi une femme, c’est peut-être la seule différence. J’ai suivi ses pas tout en gardant ma propre identité artistique. Comme lui, je chante en soussou, en malinké et en pular. La musique est une passion familiale. Où en êtes-vous aujourd’hui dans votre carrière musicale ? Cela fait deux ans que j’ai terminé un album de 13 titres dans ces trois langues. C’est une autoproduction, financée grâce aux revenus de mes prestations dans les cérémonies et les espaces de divertissement. Vous avez récemment produit un titre en hommage au président Mamadi Doumbouya. Pourquoi ce choix ? J’ai composé un morceau à la gloire du président Mamadi Doumbouya parce que j’apprécie sa gouvernance. Je suis son parcours depuis qu’il était colonel. C’est d’ailleurs pour cette raison que j’ai mis momentanément en pause la promotion de mon album. Je ne fais pas de politique, je suis artiste. Mais j’apprécie sa manière de gouverner et j’ai voulu exprimer ce sentiment à travers une chanson. Qu’attendez-vous désormais ? Hadji Sow : Mon souhait est de rencontrer le président. Je sais qu’il connaît déjà ma chanson puisqu’elle lui est parvenue sur WhatsApp. Une audience avait été programmée, mais elle n’a finalement pas eu lieu parce que les personnes qui devaient m’accompagner sont arrivées en retard. Je n’ai pas eu une autre occasion depuis. Je me dis que Dieu en a décidé ainsi. Je profite également de cette occasion pour remercier sa sœur, Aïcha Doumbouya, qui m’a accueillie à Kankan avec beaucoup de respect. Il faut savoir reconnaître les bienfaits. Quel regard portez-vous sur la nouvelle génération d’artistes guinéens ? Hadji Sow : Je trouve qu’elle fait de bonnes choses. Les artistes s’adaptent à leur époque, ce qui est normal. Mais il est aussi important de construire une véritable carrière. Un succès qui dure un ou deux mois ne suffit pas. Si l’on parle encore aujourd’hui de Seydou Sow, de Bambino et d’autres artistes, c’est parce qu’ils ont laissé des œuvres durables. On ne peut pas faire uniquement des chansons d’amour ou des morceaux liés à l’actualité. Il faut aussi produire des œuvres intemporelles et porteuses de messages. Sinon, un autre artiste viendra rapidement prendre votre place. À long terme, cela ne rapporte ni récompenses ni véritable reconnaissance. Vous défendez donc une musique qui traverse le temps, comme votre titre « Dakan » ? Exactement. Aujourd’hui, certains estiment qu’une chanson de quatre minutes est déjà trop longue parce que les gens ont moins de temps. Pourtant, il faut privilégier la qualité du message. Lorsque j’ai enregistré « Dakan » (Destin), je ne disposais que de 300 000 francs guinéens alors que les frais de studio s’élevaient à deux millions. Par générosité, les responsables du studio m’ont soutenue. Je suis convaincue que, dans dix ou vingt ans, cette chanson gardera toute sa valeur parce qu’elle véhicule un véritable message. Quels sont vos projets actuels ? Mon album est entièrement terminé. Mon objectif est désormais de réaliser un clip pour le titre phare. Si j’avais davantage de moyens, je tournerais trois clips, un dans chacune des langues de l’album. Après réflexion, j’ai choisi de commencer par « Mon Général ». Le plus difficile est déjà fait. J’espère maintenant bénéficier d’un accompagnement pour concrétiser ce projet. Quel message adressez-vous à ceux qui souhaitent vous soutenir ? Hadji Sow : M’aider sera plus facile puisque toutes les chansons sont déjà enregistrées. Elles ont été produites dans de grands studios, notamment ceux d’Ahmed Fofana et de Koudou Hatanas. Aujourd’hui, ma priorité est la réalisation du clip. Toute personne qui souhaite m’accompagner est la bienvenue. Réalisée par Marie Mayi Cissé et Robert Kourouma L’article INTERVIEW- Hadji Sow : « Je ne fais pas de politique, mais j’ai chanté le président Mamadi Doumbouya parce que j’apprécie sa gouvernance » est apparu en premier sur Mediaguinee.com .

items.title: Violences xénophobes en Afrique du Sud: plus de 160.000 personnes ont quitté le pays depuis fin mai 2026
items.description: VIOLENCES-XÉNOPHOBES-AFRIQUE-SUD-MIGRANTS-RAPATRIEMENT Violences xénophobes en Afrique du Sud: plus de 160.000 personnes ont quitté le pays depuis fin mai 2026 Ouagadougou, 23 juil. 2026 (AIB) – Plus de 160.000 ressortissants de plusieurs pays africains ont été contraints de quitter l'Afrique du Sud depuis la fin du mois de mai 2026 en raison de la vague de violences xénophobes, selon des estimations des médias. Plus de 108.300 personnes sont rentrées au Zimbabwe. Le Malawi a, pour sa part, accueilli près de 46.900 de ses ressortissants de retour. Le Nigeria et le Ghana ont rapatrié respectivement 1.490 et 926 de leurs ressortissants. En outre, plusieurs milliers de personnes ont quitté l'Afrique du Sud pour regagner le Mozambique. Des centaines d'autres sont retournées en Ouganda, au Lesotho, au Kenya et en République démocratique du Congo. D’autres ressortissants africains ont également perdu la vie lors de ces violences xénophobes en Afrique du Sud. Depuis le début de l'année 2026, des manifestations hostiles aux étrangers africains se tiennent régulièrement dans différentes régions d'Afrique du Sud. Elles sont accompagnées d'affrontements avec les forces de l'ordre, de pillages de commerces appartenant à des étrangers et de troubles à l'ordre public. Face à la dégradation de la situation, plusieurs pays africains, dont le Nigeria, le Ghana, le Malawi et le Mozambique, ont organisé le rapatriement de leurs ressortissants vivant en Afrique du Sud. Agence d'information du Burkina Sources: Tass

items.title: CAN féminine 2026 : Des observateurs croient aux chances des Étalons dames malgré un groupe relevé
items.description: À quelques jours de leur entrée en lice à la Coupe d'Afrique des nations (CAN) féminine Maroc 2026, les Étalons dames suscitent analyses et commentaires. Versées dans un groupe B relevé avec l'Afrique du Sud, la Côte d'Ivoire et la Tanzanie, les joueuses de Pascal Sawadogo devront, selon plusieurs journalistes et consultants sportifs, faire preuve de détermination et d'efficacité pour espérer décrocher une qualification au deuxième tour.p Les Étalons dames du Burkina Faso participeront, pour la deuxième fois de leur histoire, à une Coupe d'Afrique des nations (CAN) féminine. Les joueuses de Pascal Sawadogo évolueront dans le groupe B, aux côtés de la Côte d'Ivoire, de la Tanzanie et de l'Afrique du Sud. À l'approche de cette compétition, prévue du 25 juillet au 16 août 2026 au Maroc, plusieurs journalistes et consultants sportifs se sont prononcés sur les chances de qualification du Burkina Faso. Les Étalons dames feront leur entrée en lice le 27 juillet face à la Côte d'Ivoire. La 14ᵉ édition de la Coupe d'Afrique des nations féminine se disputera au Maroc du 25 juillet au 16 août 2026. Pour Ismaël Saïdou Ganamé, journaliste sportif à Faso 7, Stéphane Bassalia Ouattara, journaliste à Africa Top Sports, Aubin Ouédraogo, journaliste et consultant sportif, ainsi que Franck Aristide Gefa, journaliste à Ouaga FM, les Étalons dames évolueront dans un groupe particulièrement relevé, composé de nations plus expérimentées telles que l'Afrique du Sud et la Côte d'Ivoire. Tous s'accordent à dire que les Burkinabè devront se surpasser pour espérer franchir la phase de groupes. Pour le journaliste sportif de Faso 7, Ismaël Saïdou Ganamé, les Étalons dames, qui ne disputent que leur deuxième CAN féminine, ne figurent pas parmi les favorites de leur groupe. Selon lui, les joueuses de Pascal Sawadogo devront aller au-delà de leurs limites pour espérer accéder au tour suivant. Ismaël Saïdou Ganamé, journaliste sportif à Faso 7, estime que les joueuses de Pascal Sawadogo devront se surpasser pour franchir la phase de groupes. « Les Étalons dames sont dans le groupe B avec l'Afrique du Sud, la Côte d'Ivoire et la Tanzanie. Elles ne sont qu'à leur deuxième participation à une Coupe d'Afrique des Nations. Donc, elles ne sont pas du tout les favorites de cette poule. Je trouve que l'Afrique du Sud et la Côte d'Ivoire sont à un niveau supérieur. Je dirais que la Côte d'Ivoire est juste un ou deux crans au-dessus. Et l'Afrique du Sud, carrément, c'est un mastodonte. Je ne dirais pas que les Étalons n'ont aucune chance de se qualifier, car elles partent sur les mêmes bases. C'est zéro point pour tout le monde. Mais je vais être réaliste : les chances sont minces pour les Étalons de sortir de cette poule. Cependant, si, en plus de leur talent intrinsèque, elles arrivent à mettre du cœur, du courage, de la détermination et surtout de la volonté dans les matches qui vont les opposer aux équipes de cette poule, elles auront la possibilité de se qualifier pour le tour suivant. Déjà, elles auront la possibilité de gagner un match et, pourquoi pas, d'accrocher l'une des deux équipes que sont la Côte d'Ivoire et l'Afrique du Sud pour espérer se qualifier. Je pense donc que la bonne compétition des Étalons dames dans cette CAN va tenir à leur capacité à se surpasser », a-t-il confié. De son côté, Stéphane Bassalia Ouattara, journaliste à Africa Top Sports, estime lui aussi que les Étalons dames évoluent dans un groupe très relevé, composé de l'Afrique du Sud, de la Tanzanie et de la Côte d'Ivoire. Il reste toutefois convaincu que le Burkina Faso peut créer la surprise et décrocher son billet pour le tour suivant. « Le Burkina Faso n'en est pas à sa première participation, mais à sa deuxième, ce qui signifie qu'on a un peu d'expérience. On dispose aussi d'un groupe de qualité capable de titiller les différentes équipes qui composent cette poule. Le Burkina Faso peut créer la surprise en franchissant la phase de groupes, parce que, quand on regarde notre équipe, on constate qu'elle possède de réelles qualités individuelles. Nous avons aussi notre mot à dire. C'est surtout l'Afrique du Sud qui peut véritablement intimider le Burkina Faso, parce que, comme je le disais un peu plus tôt, ces Sud-Africaines sont très fortes. Mais ce n'est plus tout à fait la même équipe que celle que nous avons connue par le passé. Elle est un peu vieillissante et compte également de nouvelles joueuses qui n'ont pas encore une grande expérience de cette compétition. Je me dis donc qu'en dehors de cette Afrique du Sud, le Burkina Faso peut rivaliser avec la Tanzanie et la Côte d'Ivoire. C'est une Coupe d'Afrique des Nations féminine où des surprises sont possibles. Nous pouvons franchir la phase de groupes. Tout dépendra également de ce que le coach mettra en place, mais aussi du premier match face à la Côte d'Ivoire, qui sera très important », a-t-il expliqué. Stéphane Bassalia Ouattara, journaliste à Africa Top Sports, pense que le Burkina Faso peut créer la surprise et se qualifier pour le tour suivant. Pour Aubin Ouédraogo, journaliste sportif et consultant, une qualification au deuxième tour est envisageable pour les joueuses de Pascal Sawadogo, à condition de réaliser un excellent parcours lors de la phase de groupes. « Le groupe B est particulièrement relevé. L'Afrique du Sud fait figure de favorite, tandis que la Côte d'Ivoire possède une équipe expérimentée. La Tanzanie, en revanche, semble être l'adversaire le plus abordable sur le papier. Pour les filles de Pascal Sawadogo, une qualification pour le deuxième tour est possible, mais elle passera par un excellent parcours en phase de groupes. En effet, le match Burkina Faso-Côte d'Ivoire, qui est le premier, sera décisif pour la suite. Une victoire placerait les Burkinabè dans une excellente position. Un match nul resterait un bon résultat, tandis qu'une défaite compliquerait la suite », a-t-il analysé. Selon lui, le Burkina Faso a de quoi nourrir des ambitions et possède les arguments nécessaires pour rivaliser avec ses adversaires et créer la surprise dans ce groupe. Pour Aubin Ouédraogo, le Burkina Faso a de quoi nourrir des ambitions et possède les arguments nécessaires pour rivaliser dans le groupe B. Franck Aristide Gefa, journaliste sportif à Ouaga FM, considère pour sa part le Burkina Faso comme un outsider du groupe B. Selon lui, les Étalons dames auront leur mot à dire dans une poule particulièrement disputée. « Je pense que l'objectif d'ailleurs fixé et assigné aux Étalons dames, c'est bien sûr d'enregistrer une première victoire lors de cette compétition. Vous vous souvenez que, lors de leur première participation, en 2022, les Étalons avaient enregistré deux défaites et un match nul. Je pense qu'elles ont les moyens de décrocher cette première victoire. Il suffit de faire preuve de confiance, dans la mesure où elles ont récemment participé à une immersion patriotique, ce qui leur a permis de cultiver et de renforcer l'amour du drapeau national. Je pense qu'à partir de cela, elles pourront se sublimer et avoir davantage confiance en elles-mêmes », a-t-il analysé. Il estime également que les Étalons dames disposent de réelles chances de qualification et qu'elles peuvent se donner les moyens d'atteindre le deuxième tour pour leur deuxième participation à une CAN féminine. Franck Aristide Gefa, journaliste sportif à Ouaga FM, estime que le Burkina Faso fait figure d'outsider dans le groupe B. Jean Elysée Nikiéma Lefaso.net

items.title: GCX calls for mandatory commodity trading route for foreign buyers to protect Ghana’s farmers
items.description: The Ghana Commodity Exchange (GCX) has called for a policy framework that would require foreign buyers of Ghanaian agricultural commodities to purchase through the exchange rather than sourcing directly from farm gates. According to the GCX, channelling commodity purchases through the exchange will help regulate the agricultural market, improve price stability, increase government revenue, and provide better data for national planning. Speaking on Joy FM’s Super Morning Show on Thursday, July 23, Joseph Nai Anum, Managing Marketing and Business Development at the Ghana Commodity Exchange, said one of the major challenges affecting Ghana’s agricultural sector is the competition created by foreign buyers who go directly to farming communities to purchase commodities. He explained that these foreign buyers often compete with local processors and traders, creating challenges within the domestic agricultural value chain. “One of the major challenges affecting our agricultural agenda has to do with foreigners who go into our farm spaces or the farm gates to buy commodities,” he said. “They compete seriously with our local buyers and processors. We believe that if these foreign buyers are regulated and allowed to buy commodities mandatorily through the Ghana Commodity Exchange, it would help stabilise the agricultural space and also stabilise prices.” Mr Anum said the GCX’s next major objective is to facilitate the trading of export-oriented commodities such as cashews, shea nuts, and soybeans through the exchange, with support from government policy. He noted that requiring foreign buyers to trade through the GCX would bring several benefits, including improved tax collection, better price regulation, and enhanced government oversight of commodity flows. “One of the benefits is that the government will be able to collect taxes after trades from these foreigners. It will also help regulate prices and give sellers and farmers the best prices they can get,” he said. He added that the system would also provide critical data on the volume of commodities leaving the country, allowing the government to make better-informed decisions on agricultural policies and planning. “When these foreigners are trading, we get to know how much is being taken out of the country, and the government can use that information in planning within the agricultural space,” he explained. GCX Encourages Farmers and Businesses to Use Exchange Platform The Head of Operations at the Ghana Commodity Exchange, Wendy Malm, encouraged farmers, processors and businesses to make greater use of the exchange to improve access to markets, storage facilities, and financing opportunities. She said farmers who bring their commodities to GCX warehouses will benefit from secure storage facilities, including insurance coverage to protect their produce. “We would want to encourage everyone to patronise and use the Ghana Commodity Exchange. For farmers, they can bring their commodities for storage in the GCX warehouses,” she said. “This will ensure that the commodities are well stored. We also have insurance on all our warehouses, so we will ensure that your commodities are safe and sound.” Mrs Malm explained that farmers could also access finance through the warehouse receipt financing system while benefiting from competitive prices through the GCX trading platform. She also urged buyers, including food producers and animal feed manufacturers, to source commodities through the exchange to guarantee access to quality grains in the right quantities. “Whether you need commodities for food production or animal feed production, you will be able to have access to quality grains and the right quantity, including large volumes, at every point in time,” she said. GCX Promotes Safer Local Food Consumption Mr Anum also encouraged consumers of local staples such as kenkey and banku to purchase commodities traded through the Ghana Commodity Exchange, stressing that the platform promotes food safety standards. He said commodities traded through the exchange are tested to ensure quality, including protection against harmful substances such as aflatoxin. The Ghana Commodity Exchange, established to improve transparency and efficiency in Ghana’s agricultural markets, provides a platform where farmers, traders, processors and buyers can trade commodities under a regulated system.

items.title: Zapatero baja de la Luna
items.description: Zapatero en la televisión pública con Javier Ruiz. La entrevista en sí ya encierra un debate: ¿le conviene? Refugiado hasta ahora en el silencio, el expresidente decide coger el guante mediático y presentar batalla pública. Pero llega con esa batalla empezada, así que lo único que puede hacer ya es defenderse, a veces despejando en línea de meta. Su gesto más repetido es el encogimiento de hombros para expresar desconcierto, asombro o locura por las acusaciones que carga. Ruiz le presenta con obstinación la realidad (mensajes, pruebas de la UDEF, citas, comidas, extrañas casualidades de fechas) y Zapatero se revuelve agitando las manos primero, apuntando con el dedo después: “Quien insinúa y acusa tiene que probarlo, no yo, si no existe aquello de que se me acusa”. Seguir leyendo

items.title: Zapatero, sobre el rescate de Plus Ultra: “No es que no influyera, es que no hablé con nadie”
items.description: La primera entrevista de José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero —en el programa Mañaneros de TVE— desde que la Audiencia Nacional le imputó por liderar una trama de tráfico de influencias ilícitas, ha dejado escasas novedades de impacto jurídico. El propio expresidente del Gobierno ha admitido durante su intervención que, tras dos meses en silencio –más allá de un breve vídeo difundido para declarar su inocencia–, a su delicada situación con la justicia se le ha sumado “un problema paralelo”: su “necesidad” de dar explicaciones porque se lo reclaman amigos y compañeros del partido. Bajo esa premisa, se ha centrado en reiterar en público lo que ya sostuvo a puerta cerrada ante el juez José Luis Calama cuando declaró como investigado el 17 de junio: que ni intervino en el rescate de Plus Ultra, ni planeó la creación de una empresa offshore para camuflar sus ingresos, ni usó a sus hijas de testaferros. Seguir leyendo

items.title: Downstream sector needs policy certainty, investment to drive growth – CBOD CEO
items.description: The Chief Executive Officer of the Ghana Chamber of Bulk Oil Distributors (CBOD), Dr Patrick Kwaku Ofori, has called for stable policies, greater innovation, and sustained investment to strengthen Ghana’s downstream petroleum industry and position the country as the leading petroleum and logistics hub in West Africa. Speaking at the opening of the 7th Ghana International Petroleum Conference (GhIPCon 2026) in Accra, Dr Ofori said the country’s downstream petroleum sector remains a critical pillar of economic growth, providing the energy that powers industries, agriculture, healthcare, transportation, and commerce. Addressing participants under the theme “Building a Resilient Downstream: Policy, Innovation, and Investment for Growth,” he said the sector must adapt to an increasingly complex global environment characterised by geopolitical tensions, supply chain disruptions, exchange rate volatility, and rapidly evolving energy markets. “The downstream petroleum sector does far more than distribute fuel. It keeps our economy moving. It powers our industries, supports our farmers, enables healthcare, facilitates trade, and connects communities across the country. Every day, millions of Ghanaians depend on a downstream sector that is reliable, efficient, and responsive,” he said. According to him, recent global developments have reinforced the need for resilience across the petroleum value chain. “Resilience is no longer simply about responding to crises. It is about anticipating them, preparing for them, adapting to them, and emerging stronger because of them. Resilience is not measured by how we perform when conditions are favourable; it is measured by how prepared we are when they are not.” Dr Ofori identified policy certainty, technological innovation, and infrastructure investment as the three critical pillars needed to build a resilient downstream industry. On policy, he stressed that predictable regulations and consistent government policies remain essential to attracting both local and foreign investment. “Investors value certainty. Businesses thrive where policies are consistent, regulations are transparent, and institutions are predictable. A stable policy environment encourages investment, promotes healthy competition, protects consumers, and gives businesses the confidence to plan for the future.” He noted that good policy should go beyond regulation by creating an environment that enables businesses to innovate, expand, and remain competitive. Touching on innovation, Dr Ofori said digital transformation is rapidly reshaping the petroleum industry and urged companies to embrace emerging technologies. “From digital systems and smarter logistics to automation, predictive analytics, artificial intelligence, and cleaner technologies, innovation is helping us improve efficiency, strengthen safety, reduce costs and make better decisions. It is no longer a competitive advantage; it is a business necessity.” He further called for strategic investments in petroleum infrastructure to enhance energy security and improve operational efficiency. “We must continue investing in modern storage facilities, strategic petroleum reserves, local refining capacity, transport corridors, and the road networks that connect our ports, depots and markets. These investments will improve supply reliability, lower logistics costs, create jobs, stimulate industrialisation and strengthen Ghana’s energy security.” Beyond meeting domestic demand, Dr. Ofori said Ghana should pursue a broader ambition of becoming the preferred downstream petroleum and logistics hub for West Africa. “Our goal should not simply be to keep pace with change. Our goal should be to position Ghana as the downstream petroleum and logistics hub for West Africa, a country recognised for reliability, efficiency, innovation, and investment. That ambition is achievable if the government and the private sector continue to work together with a shared sense of purpose.” He also underscored the importance of balancing economic growth with environmental sustainability, arguing that cleaner fuels and responsible investments are essential to Ghana’s long-term energy transition. “Economic growth and environmental responsibility are not competing priorities; they are mutually reinforcing. Through cleaner fuels, improved operational practices, lower-emission technologies, and responsible investments, the downstream sector can make a meaningful contribution to Ghana’s energy transition while continuing to power economic development.” Dr. Ofori reaffirmed CBOD’s commitment to collaborating with government, regulators, development partners and industry stakeholders to build a stronger and more resilient petroleum sector. “Building resilience is not the responsibility of any one institution. It is a shared responsibility that demands collaboration, trust, innovation, and sustained commitment from all of us.” He urged participants to ensure that discussions at the two-day conference translate into practical actions capable of strengthening the industry and enhancing Ghana’s competitiveness. “The future of Ghana’s downstream petroleum industry will not be shaped by circumstance. It will be shaped by the decisions we make, the partnerships we build, and the investments we choose to make today.” The 7th Ghana International Petroleum Conference brought together policymakers, regulators, oil marketing companies, bulk oil distributors, investors, and development partners to deliberate on strategies for building a more resilient, competitive, and sustainable downstream petroleum sector capable of supporting Ghana’s long-term economic transformation.

items.title: Le consulat du Maroc à Bologne suit de près l'affaire du décès du ressortissant marocain Abderrahim Fakir
items.description: Le consulat général du Maroc à Bologne a affirmé continuer de suivre, en étroite coordination avec l'ambassade du Royaume à Rome, l'affaire du décès du ressortissant marocain Abderrahim Fakir, survenu le 19 juillet à Bologne lors d'une intervention des forces de police italiennes. Dans ce cadre, la consule générale, Khadija Nador, s'est rendue mercredi auprès de la famille du défunt, sur instructions du ministre des Affaires étrangères, de la Coopération africaine et des Marocains résidant à l'étranger, afin de lui faire part de la pleine mobilisation des services diplomatiques et consulaires du Royaume pour assurer le suivi de cette affaire et mettre en œuvre tous les moyens nécessaires en vue de fournir l'assistance ainsi que l'accompagnement administratif et consulaire requis en ces circonstances tragiques, indique un communiqué du consulat général du Maroc à Bologne. La représentation consulaire, en coordination étroite avec l'ambassade du Royaume à Rome, poursuit également les contacts directs avec les autorités italiennes compétentes et suit de très près les différentes étapes de la procédure judiciaire en cours, dans le plein respect des dispositions juridiques en vigueur, en attendant l'achèvement des investigations et des procédures judiciaires, souligne la même source. Le consulat ajoute qu'il continue aussi d'accompagner la famille du défunt dans l'ensemble des démarches administratives et consulaires liées au rapatriement de la dépouille du défunt vers le Maroc, dès l'achèvement des procédures juridiques et l'obtention des autorisations nécessaires de la part des autorités italiennes compétentes. Dans ce contexte, l'ambassade du Maroc à Rome et le consulat général du Royaume à Bologne ont réaffirmé leur engagement à continuer à suivre cette affaire avec toute l'attention et le sens des responsabilités requis, et à fournir à la famille du défunt les différentes formes d'assistance et d'accompagnement nécessaires. SS The post Le consulat du Maroc à Bologne suit de près l'affaire du décès du ressortissant marocain Abderrahim Fakir appeared first on Hespress Français - Actualités du Maroc .

items.title: Sessions judge, his guard killed after vehicles targeted en route to Mastung
items.description: QUETTA: District and Sessions Judge Abdul Hakeem Kakar and his guard were gunned down on Thursday, while another judge was among two injured when they came under attack while heading to Mastung, officials said. Judge Kakar and Additional Sessions Judge Tariq Lashari were on their way to Mastung from Quetta when unknown armed men attacked the vehicles they were in, Balochistan Home Department’s aide Babar Yousafzai told Dawn . The armed men targeted Kakar and others at Wali Khan crossing, killing him and his guard on the spot, Yousafzai said. He added that the two injured, including Lashari, were taken to a hospital in Mastung. Balochistan Chief Minister Sarfraz Bugti has taken notice of the incident, his aide for political affairs Shahid Rind told Geo News . “All aspects will be considered. If anyone’s negligence is found at any point, they will not be spared,” he asserted. Referring to the killing, Rind said, “A judge was targeted only because he is from the judicial fraternity.” “The BYC (Baloch Yakjehti Committee) leadership was sentenced in Quetta, but where was the judge targeted? In Mastung,” he noted. “The terrorists want to give a message that ‘if you give verdicts against us, we will target you’.” Rind cautioned against “political confusions”, stressing the need to respond to terrorists with force “without any confusion”. The official voiced support for “ faceless courts ”. Condemnations President Asif Ali Zardari strongly condemned the attack and expressed his grief over the deaths of the judge and his guard. “The sacrifices of the martyrs will not go in vain,” he said in a statement, praying for the speedy recovery of the injured. “The nefarious aims of anti-state elements active under external patronage will never succeed,” the president asserted. He reaffirmed that Pakistan would continue its counterterrorism efforts till the “complete eradication of the menace of terrorism”. In his condemnation, Interior Minister Mohsin Naqvi extended his heartfelt condolences to the bereaved families, state-run APP reported. The minister said such “cowardly acts cannot weaken the nation’s unwavering resolve”. He reaffirmed the government’s solidarity with the families of the martyrs, stating, “We stand with the families of the martyrs and will continue to stand by them at all times.” PPP Chairman Bilawal Bhutto-Zardari, whose party is in power in the province, also denounced the killing. “I am sure the terrorists involved in this cowardly attack will soon be brought to justice,” he said in a statement. Bilawal affirmed that the PPP and the judiciary stood with the people of Balochistan and the security forces fighting terrorism. Spike in terror incidents Thursday’s incident came a day after three people were killed and five others were injured in a gun attack on a Karachi-bound passenger bus and a bridge on the national highway was blown up in Mastung’s Khud Kocha area. Subsequently, authorities imposed an indefinite curfew in the Khud Kocha sub-tehsil from 9pm last night. The attack also follows security forces killing three terrorists this week in Mastung, where the Inter-Services Public Relations (ISPR) said two suicide bombers and their facilitator were also apprehended. Another seven terrorists were eliminated in Surab district. Security concerns have heightened in Balochistan after a spate of major terrorist attacks took place earlier this month, which prompted counterterrorism operations across the province. Among the counterterrorism efforts was Operation Shaban , in which 91 terrorists were killed between July 5 and July 16, according to security sources cited by state media. This took the tally of terrorists eliminated in operations across the province since July 5 to 129.

items.title: GUTA welcomes no new taxes plan ahead of 2026 Mid-Year Budget Review
items.description: The Ghana Union of Traders’ Associations (GUTA) President, Clement Boateng, has welcomed reports that the 2026 Mid-Year Budget Review will not introduce any new taxes , describing the move as a major relief for businesses already grappling with a heavy tax burden. His comments were in response to the Technical Advisor at the Ministry of Finance, Dr Theophilius Acheampong, who said the 2026 Mid-Year Budget Review will focus on the government’s fiscal performance, expenditure, borrowing, and key policy initiatives, with no plans to introduce new taxes. Speaking on JoyFM’s Midday News on Thursday, July 23, ahead of the Finance Minister’s presentation, GUTA President said the decision, if confirmed, would be good news for the business community. “I think it is welcome news because the business community is already overburdened with a lot of taxes. So it is good news that no more taxes are going to be added,” he said. He added, “What we have always been saying is for them to broaden the tax base so that a lot more people could be brought into the tax net, rather than those who are already paying taxes continually being burdened with more taxes.” According to him, widening the tax base would improve domestic revenue mobilisation without placing additional pressure on businesses that are already fulfilling their tax obligations.

items.title: Lagarde mantiene tipos y deja la siguiente subida para septiembre: “El shock energético puede intensificarse”
items.description: El precio del petróleo vuelve a tocar los 100 dólares el barril y el del gas natural en Europa se dispara al nivel más alto desde que empezó la guerra de Irán . El coste de la energía vuelve a ser un motivo de preocupación en la zona euro pero el Banco Central Europeo (BCE) ha decidido este jueves no avanzar en las subidas de tipos y dejarlos en el 2,25%, después de que en junio ejecutara el primer incremento en tres años . El renovado encarecimiento de los combustibles a causa del conflicto bélico en Oriente Próximo promete seguir alimentando la inflación, una vez enterrada la tregua vista en junio en la guerra y también en los precios. El Consejo de Gobierno del BCE ha optado en su reunión celebrada en Fráncfort por hacer una pausa aunque lo hace casi con resignación, asumiendo que el impacto del shock energético va a durar meses —si es que no va a más— y que aún llevará algo de tiempo ver la plenitud de sus efectos. Así, el terreno está preparado para una nueva subida de los tipos en septiembre que los inversores prevén ya de forma unánime. Seguir leyendo

items.title: Google hit with $1bn EU fine, in 'constructive' talks to avoid more penalties
items.description: Alphabet’s Google was fined a total of €890 million ($1 billion) on Thursday for flouting European Union rules aimed at reining in the power of Big Tech, the European Commission said. However, the US tech giant is likely to avoid fresh fines as EU regulators lauded good progress in its ongoing efforts to comply with the landmark legislation. The fines underscored Europe’s determination to prevent Big Tech companies from thwarting rivals, defying US criticism and retaliatory tariff threats. One fine of €460 million was handed out to Google by the European Commission under the Digital Markets Act for favouring its own services in shopping, hotels, transport and sports results in search results. A second fine of €430 million targeted Google’s restrictions on its app store Google Play, preventing app developers from steering users free of charge to cheaper offers on rival app stores or websites. Reuters was the first to report on both fines, which are the first for the US tech giant under the DMA but the fifth and sixth overall for anti-competitive practices, making for total penalties of €10.38 billion over nearly two decades. “Our duty and obligation is to comply with the laws, [so] that our laws are fully respected,” EU antitrust chief Teresa Ribera told reporters when asked about US pushback. “The DMA is to make sure we have a fair and level playing field. With these decisions we want to make sure there is competition,” EU tech chief Henna Virkkunen told reporters. Google, which has 60 days to comply with the Commission’s orders to treat rivals in a fair and non-discriminatory manner and to allow app developers to steer users away from its app store, criticised the EU findings and said it might take the Commission to court. “To comply, we are having to strip away real-time search features Europeans love — like instant pricing and direct availability for hotels, flights, and restaurants - and dismantle safety protections on Google Play,” Google President of Global Affairs Kent Walker said in a statement. “This isn’t fair competition; it’s product degradation driven by a small group of self-serving complainants, with European businesses and consumers taking the hit. Regulation should improve products, not make them worse,” he said. More fines unlikely given ‘constructive dialogue’ The Commission, which acts as the EU competition enforcer, pointed to a “constructive dialogue” with Google and significant progress made to comply with the DMA, indicating that daily penalties for non-compliance are likely off the table. “Google has proposed and started testing changes to how it presents its own services on Google Search for free services such as shopping, hotels and flights,” the Commission said, calling it substantial progress. “The Commission also notes that Google has proposed and started testing changes to how it presents shopping ads and content-related services, such as sports,” it said, adding it would assess the changes and continue talks with Google. The EU watchdog also said Google may apply the principles of Thursday’s decision to its AI-generated summaries known as AI Overviews and AI Mode and that talks would continue to this end. Google’s changes to its steering terms on Google Play received a tentative thumbs up from the Commission. “These constitute good progress towards compliance and will also be assessed in light of the cease and desist order of today’s decision,” it said. Europe’s crackdown on Big Tech has angered U.S. President Donald Trump’s administration, which has threatened to retaliate with tariffs for what he said are moves targeting US companies, while US lawmakers have also piled on the pressure. The fines are the third under the DMA after penalties handed out to Apple and Meta Platforms in April last year.

items.title: KNUST Pharmacy Dean warns against rising self-medication and drug abuse in Ghana
items.description: The Head of the Faculty of Pharmacy and Pharmaceutical Sciences at the Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology (KNUST) , Professor Isaac Ayensu, has raised concerns over the increasing practice of self-medication and drug abuse in Ghana, warning that it poses significant public health risks. Prof. Ayensu said the indiscriminate use of medicines without proper medical guidance could have long-term consequences on the health of individuals and the country as a whole. Speaking ahead of the faculty’s 65th anniversary celebration , he urged Ghanaians to seek professional advice from pharmacists and other healthcare providers before taking medications. “Every drug that you take is a potential poison. Drugs must be rationalised and should target specific conditions that people are suffering from,” he cautioned. He explained that many people resort to self-medication because they believe they understand their health conditions, but stressed the importance of obtaining proper medical advice before using drugs. “A lot of people go for medication because they believe they understand their conditions, but it is important that we visit the pharmacy and seek medical advice when we are unwell,” he said. Prof. Ayensu noted that pharmacists have an important role in ensuring the safe and effective use of medicines but warned that individuals must avoid self-diagnosis and the misuse of drugs. He said failure to address the growing trend could negatively affect national health outcomes in the future. Pressure on pharmacy training facilities Beyond concerns over drug misuse, Prof. Ayensu highlighted challenges facing the faculty, particularly inadequate laboratory infrastructure for practical training. He said the increasing number of students admitted annually has outgrown available facilities, making it difficult to provide the level of hands-on training required for pharmacy education. “At the moment, what really is the issue is that the growing number of students admitted each year would need a certain calibre of training which goes beyond the ordinary classroom and that would mean they have to be accommodated within laboratories that are able to capture a lot of students at a time,” he stated. According to him, the limited laboratory space has forced the faculty to schedule practical sessions in batches, reducing opportunities for students to gain extensive practical experience. “If we can have a laboratory which is big enough to accommodate more than 50 or 100 students at a time, it will be a wonderful exposure where they are able to gain a lot of experiences that they want to get,” he added. 65 years of pharmacy education The Faculty of Pharmacy and Pharmaceutical Sciences at KNUST, Ghana’s pioneer pharmacy school, is marking its 65th anniversary with a series of activities organised by the Ghana Pharmaceutical Students’ Association (GPSA)-KNUST . The celebration is being held under the theme: “65 Years of Leadership: Consolidating Gains, Inspiring Generations.” President of GPSA-KNUST, Albert Kobina Yalley , also raised concerns about the growing student population and its impact on the association’s ability to provide support and organise programmes. “The growing membership of GPSA-KNUST continues to increase the needs of student pharmacists, thereby stretching our available resources and expertise in implementing impactful programmes for students,” he said. However, Mr Yalley expressed optimism about the future of pharmacy education and practice in Ghana, saying the association’s goal is to develop future leaders in the pharmaceutical sector. “Our vision is that within the next five years, GPSA-KNUST students will be recognised as leaders, pioneers and pillars of Ghana’s pharmaceutical industry,” he added.

items.title: Las llamadas al 016 crecieron un 120,8% en las horas posteriores a que España ganara el Mundial
items.description: El pasado domingo por la noche, a las nueve, se jugó la final del Mundial de fútbol. España ganó a Argentina . items.title: Is the Age of Reading Over?
items.description: Subscribe here: Apple Podcasts | Spotify | YouTube | Overcast | Pocket Casts Over the past 18 months, scholars have debated—and likely will continue to long into the future—why voters returned Donald Trump to the presidency. One intriguing theory: It’s because Americans don’t read books anymore. Fewer than half of all adults reported reading a book of any kind in 2022; only 38 percent read a novel or short story. And the proportion of Americans reading for pleasure on any given day is plummeting, according to the latest Atlantic cover story, “ The End of Reading Is Here ,” by Rose Horowitch. We are all awash in text, of course: messages from friends, headlines, captions on videos. But not the kinds of rich, layered sentences that have shaped our brains for centuries and given rise to many academic disciplines. Hence, we are a populace susceptible to a man who can capture attention, stir emotion, and breezily contradict himself, because his words are not etched in stone, or anywhere, really. Horowitch joins Radio Atlantic to discuss what it means to live in a postliterate society, what we’ve lost, and how we might recover. The following is a transcript of the episode: Hanna Rosin: We’re going to have a conversation about the end of reading today. This idea, proposed in the most recent Atlantic cover story, is that the age of reading is turning out to be a short anomaly in human history and that we are fast entering a postliterate society. But before we do that, I want you to think about the arc of your own relationship with books. How did you read as a kid? In school? When did that change? Did you notice it change? What’s the last book you read, absorbed, and enjoyed? [ Music ] Rosin: I’m Hanna Rosin. This is Radio Atlantic . I’m guessing that you’ve heard someone lament about how students—in middle school, high school, or even in college—don’t read whole books anymore. Maybe you have a teacher friend who complains about having to assign excerpts or increasingly simple texts. Maybe you are a young student, and books seem annoying and time-consuming to you. Or maybe you’re an adult and just find yourself trying to read but instead reaching for your phone every couple of pages. The argument we’re discussing today is much broader than mere phone distraction and spans back to ancient Egypt. It’s about how reading shaped the human mind and then human society, and how that’s now veering in a different direction. Rosin: Do you think more people actually read your full article or heard about the debate on TikTok? Rose Horowitch: I hope that people read it, but I don’t know. Rosin: I’m talking today to the author of that cover story, Rose Horowitch, who is not the elderly professor type that you might normally assume wrote an Atlantic story called “ The End of Reading Is Here .” Horowitch: It’s so funny because I initially had a joke in there, like, You probably think I’m retirement age, reading this whole piece . Rosin: Rose is actually in her 20s. Horowitch: Yes. Rosin: She did grow up learning to love to read. But then as she got older, she found it harder. Horowitch: That’s a story probably a lot of people can relate to, of reading much more than when you were young and then gradually noticing it disappear. I think I was noticing more so when I would try to read a book that I would just, after every couple pages, want to check my phone in case I missed a notification. Or I would take a look at my watch to make sure that I didn’t have any notifications. So I had to start putting my phone in drawers or putting it in another room so that I really could not check it. So that was more what I noticed, yeah. Rosin: Right. So that’s your experience, and from your article, I’m seeing that’s the experience of lots of people. So just lay out the basics. In what ways and among whom has reading books declined? Horowitch: So reading books has declined very broadly, even among the groups of people that, you know, have been the most stalwart readers: Women and retirees and college graduates, they’re seeing a decline. We know that in the past two decades, the share of Americans that read a book or an article on any given day has declined by 40 percent. And that, as of 2022, which is the last year that we have really great data for, fewer than half of all Americans read a book at all in the year, and only 38 percent read a novel or short story. So we know that reading is just really in decline kind of across demographic groups. Rosin: And is it the types of books people now read, has that changed as well? I’m feeling very superior now ’cause I just finished my book last night, but anyway, keep going. ( Laughs .) Horowitch: ( Laughs .) Rosin: Yeah. Horowitch: So the types of books—we know that when people do read, the books that they’re reading are shorter and simpler than they used to be. New York Times best sellers have sentences that are about one-third shorter than they were a century ago. And I spoke with the head of the New York Public Library, and he was saying that young-adult fiction has really become one of their most popular offerings, even among people who are not young adults. So we’re definitely seeing that when people do pick up a book, the book that they read is shorter and the sentences are simpler and the vocabulary’s simpler, and that’s a real change as well. Rosin: You’ve written a lot about the decline of reading. I feel like it’s your subbeat now. ( Laughs .) Horowitch: Yes. Rosin: When you were doing research for this particular story, was there statistics or a comment from a teacher or something that really stuck with you or surprised you? Horowitch: I think some of the things that have been most chilling for me in reporting this out were the things that were really about children reading. And so we know that only 2 percent of American adults read to a child on any given day. And we do see that kind of every year older a child gets, the less they like to read. I spoke with one researcher who did focus groups, and high-school students described reading for pleasure as an alien practice. And so that really stuck out in my mind, just like, Okay, how did we get to this point, when kids view reading this way? Rosin: The way this argument is framed—and you just used the word chilling —is: There’s a problem. Who should we blame for the problem? Or a lot of people will read the article that way, and some will immediately go to Kids today, they’re always on their phones, and this generation of students . Some will say schools just gave up, and it’s this iteration of education that’s the problem. Do you, in your head, have a clear answer for who to blame? Horowitch: Well, it’s difficult to know exactly how much each factor plays into it. I definitely don’t think that it’s just kids these days . Rosin: That’s why I’m glad you wrote this story— Horowitch: Right. Rosin: —and not the retiring professor. Right. Horowitch: Yes. As a member of Gen Z, I’m like, If it’s kids these days, it’s me . But I think this is a story of technologies crowding out the time that people would’ve once spent reading books. We know that when the television was first invented, American households were spending tons of time watching that. Then obviously streaming, internet, smartphone, social media—it all just takes up so much of our time now that might have previously been devoted to reading a book. Rosin: We’re in some ways inundated with text. I feel on a daily basis I read a much higher quantity of actual words than I used to. So what is the difference? Horowitch: Yes, exactly. So people often talk about this as a literacy crisis, and I don’t think that that’s right at all. Our literacy rate is historically very high. We are probably reading more words than ever with social media, the number of emails you get for your job, and text messages with friends and everything. And so the difference is that we’re almost swimming in so much text that we just don’t actually have the time or, sort of, attention to kind of focus for an extended period on a rich or complex work, because our attention is turning to so many different things. And so that’s why I called it really a postliterate age instead of an illiterate age. Rosin: Explain postliterate . That’s a really important word in your argument. What does postliterate mean? Horowitch: Right. So what I was trying to get across is that reading is not just one mode of communication or one skill among many. When reading and writing first appeared about 6,000 years ago, it transformed society, and it changed the way people thought and politics and culture. And so I was trying to get across that I think that we’re living through a civilizational shift on that scale, where we’re gonna see changes in our consciousness, changes in the candidates we elect, and what our culture values, and even how we tell the story of our civilization. So that was what I was trying to get across by postliterate , that we can still read, but it’s an age where text is just not the kind of medium that we use to spread information or knowledge or culture or entertainment, and that that will have really far-reaching effects. Rosin: That was an excellent and fast summary, but I also feel like one thing about this argument is: It can seem like an elite argument that you and I and the people who read The Atlantic care about. It’s sort of ironic that you write—I don’t know how many words it was—a 7,000-word, very-well-written piece to explain how your generation doesn’t really read or have an attention span anymore. So I actually wanna dig in a little more slowly and deeply to why reading is important, ’cause we can summarize it quickly and just say to people reading is important. But I think until you actually understand the shifts that happened in human evolution or our brain via reading, it doesn’t seem that important. I can be like, I read what I want. Leave me alone . So maybe we’ll start with the work of Walter Ong. Walter Ong: Now, I understand that my task is to try to talk to you. Would you mind if I took my jacket off? It’s a little bit warmer in here— Rosin: I love his work because he’s a priest, so everything he writes about and thinks about has a spiritual dimension. He was also a teacher. So talk about his work around oral traditions versus written traditions and how they change the brain. Horowitch: Right. So I think the one thing that Ong really understood was that writing text is just a very different process than speaking it. Ong: Writing has reinforced the old. It’s made talkers talk more, but it’s made them talk differently. [ Music ] Horowitch: We tend to use more complex sentence structures and vocabulary. When you write something down, it gives you distance because you can see it reflected back at you. So you can go back to it and get new understanding. You can kind of take on a more complex analytical task. It’s a more dispassionate spread of information because you don’t immediately see the person who wrote it at the same time. Ong: It was the first time it ever occurred to him that every one of those definitions in the dictionary was something that somebody said this word means. The word didn’t come equipped with it. Somebody had to figure that out and say, This is what it means . Horowitch: And so he was arguing that it’s just very different and that this ushers in a sort of entirely new mode of thinking, a kind of rational, linear, analytical way of thinking. And that that is a precondition for a lot of the modern types of study that we think about today. Philosophy and modern science and history as an academic discipline, that those things required reading and writing. Ong: They can learn facts, but they cannot learn how to think in the way in which you have to think. The thought patterns that you have to be able to use cannot be mastered without writing. Rosin: Yeah, there’s this quote you reference from Amusing Ourselves to Death , the 1985 Neil Postman book: “Writing freezes speech.” And I think what he meant by that is that speech happens fluidly, but writing inserts a pause; and that pause allows us to reflect, shape our thoughts; and all this metacognition is happening when you write that doesn’t necessarily happen in speech, because speech is constantly moving. Is it something like that? Because I never actually thought in detail about what reading does to your brain versus writing. Horowitch: Yeah, so I think there’s two elements to it. One is that we just speak a lot faster than we write. And so it forces you to slow down and think a little bit more about what you’re putting out there. And then at the same time, when it is frozen before you, you can see it reflected back at you, this message, and so you can return to it for new understanding, think about how to reorder it, edit it, all these things that we can’t really do with speech, ’cause it just disappears into the ether. And so it sort of allows for this kind of logical way of thinking, and that, yeah, that quote from Postman really stuck out to me too. Rosin: I’m just realizing that podcasting, therefore, is for Neanderthals because— ( Laughs .) Horowitch: No. No, because it’s secondary orality. It’s mediated through this kind of writing, ’cause we’re talking about writing. Rosin: Okay. Secondary orality. I’ll take it. Okay, so that’s the long period that we’ve been through now, where we’ve evolved our brains, evolved a lot of human intellectual disciplines via reading, and now we’re entering towards postliteracy. What are the characteristics of a postliterate society, as opposed to the literate society you just explained? Horowitch: Yeah, so as part of my reporting for this piece, I interviewed a lot of cognitive scientists and was kind of trying to understand, Okay, what does happen to our consciousness? And I think what we see is that we know from neuroscience that what you practice is what your brain gets good at. And so as we’re reading less, we’re going to have less background knowledge to aid in comprehension. We’re going to have a less rational, analytical mode of thinking. We’re more accustomed to switching our attention and less accustomed to a kind of linear, extended focus on something. Even two decades ago, the average attention span on a screen was two-and-a-half minutes. And five years ago, it was 47 seconds. Rosin: There are some times when you describe it dispassionately, it almost sounds like a superpower, you know? Because speed is often thought of as good. We test students on how quickly they can do things. Tests are always timed. So I don’t know, just as you were talking, I was thinking, Could speed be some kind of intellectual superpower that we just don’t understand yet? Horowitch: Right. Well, I was trying to leave open that possibility. We do see, even as we see on tests, that it appears that IQs have been declining for the past two decades. At the same time, we are doing better on some forms of spatial rotation. So I definitely wanted to leave open the possibility that there could be these cognitive benefits that we just don’t understand and that this is a trade-off, but what we get will end up being worth it. And I think it’s too soon to tell if that’s possible. [ Music ] Rosin: After the break, who might thrive in a postliterate society? A clue: He’s currently president. [ Break ] Rosin: Okay, so that’s on the level of, like, an individual brain. There’s also an entire civilization because you start— Horowitch: ( Laughs .) Rosin: ( Laughs .) —the story at a broad civilization level. We can start at the easiest place, is with Trump. You describe him as the ideal president for a postliterate society. What does that mean? Horowitch: Yeah, so what I meant by that was that it’s sort of difficult to imagine Trump being elected in a country where information is primarily spread through text. But he really understands how to kind of grab attention and keep it in this postliterate age. So we see that he actually has kind of retrieved a lot of conventions from preliterate times. He contradicts himself as though there’s no record of what he said before. [ Music ] President Trump (in May): You’ll never have a leak. It’s very strong. If you had a knife, you can’t even cut it, it’s so strong, so powerful. It’s like powerful rubber. Reporter (in June): National Guard and police have been all over the Mall. How would these vandals have gotten so close to do something like that? Trump (in June): We didn’t have a lot of them then. Who would think that somebody would go into a pool and take a knife and start cutting it? Horowitch: He speaks with a lot of emotion, you know, often anger, and that’s just easier to remember than when someone gives a sort of dispassionate speech. Trump: I think it’s a disgrace. And I say that, and I say that, and that’s something that Nazi Germany would have done and did do. I think it’s a disgrace. Horowitch: We also see that he uses these kind of epithets that almost call back to Homer in the way that the poet described people. He says, “Sleepy Joe,” “Crooked Hillary,” and that’s just way easier to remember and repeat. And you just start to associate it. Trump: I call him Crooked Joe and Sleepy Joe. I find them to be equal actually. I do polls. Which is better: Crooked Joe ? Or Sleepy Joe ? And they poll about even. Horowitch: So we see that Trump has kind of understood how to entertain people, go viral, present things as very simple and authentic, and that’s helped him get elected. Rosin: The piece you wrote caused quite a stir, and especially among people who love books. People who love books tend to read The Atlantic , so maybe we’re getting a skewed sample. But let’s just say it caused quite a stir in the book-reading public, especially the publishing world. Is there any pushback that you’ve received that you think is interesting? Horowitch: Well, I think it’s interesting—there are a lot of positive signs for reading, which I did try to indicate in the story. Print-book sales were higher last year than they were a decade ago, and so that is certainly a fair critique. But at the same time, I tried to indicate in the piece, when I noted that, that what we’re seeing is that the people who are devoted to text are perhaps even more devoted to it than ever before. They spend more time reading than they used to, but they’re just becoming a smaller and smaller minority in society. Rosin: One thing I was thinking about is the democratization of text. When you wrote about people reading shorter sentences or YA novels, maybe one way of looking at this is that there used to be gatekeepers, people who would tell us what was literary, what was considered good to read, whereas now maybe you read what you want, which might be Colleen Hoover, which might be fan fiction, which might be someone’s online manifesto. And is that necessarily bad in the same way everything’s been democratized? Because if more people are buying books, it just means maybe they’re buying not literary books, but books. Horowitch: No, and that—I think that is a fair critique. And it sort of fits in with audiobooks almost, that they make it more accessible to read. And so I definitely do think it is a good thing if stories and information has been democratized and are accessible to more people. I think the challenge is just: Even as these things have become more widely available, we are still seeing a huge decline in reading. So it’s like, have people taken advantage of them? It’s not clear to me. Rosin: Yeah. And is your worry that because you write so much about reading inside the educational system, that down the road people will even stop reading those kinds of books because reading is just so much less fluidly integrated into education at all levels? Horowitch: Definitely we see in schools that teachers are just assigning far fewer books. I think a survey from last year found that most middle- and high-school teachers assign between zero and four books a year. And so we do see a big change where there’s a lot more emphasis on excerpts and on reading-comprehension drills. I guess my concern would be: You want everyone to have the option to read any work that they would want to, and so just making sure that students are equipped with the skills and mode of attention and practice that they can read whatever they want to instead of having some of it be unavailable to them. Rosin: Right. And I suppose there’s also a return to the analog, like reading retreats and that kind of thing. So there’s some kind of self-conscious niche, like I’m a reader . So it’s almost like your identity is you are a reader, and you go out to a reading club, and that’s who you are. It’s not everybody. It’s not a sign of a literate society. But it is your people. Horowitch: Yeah. One thing that really struck me in that Postman book was that before radio and television was invented, there was no way to be a reader, because there was no way to not be a reader. If you knew how to read, you had to. That was the only way to get information. And so it’s only now that being a reader is optional, that it really can be an identity marker. And we are seeing with some young people that there really is this drive towards more analog technologies. It seems to be a backlash to living in a digital age where everything feels ephemeral. I spoke with the head of the Cleveland Public Library, and he was saying that the young people that come in, they want a print book because they want something that feels tangible and concrete and that they can hold. So there certainly is that countervailing sign. Rosin: I know. It’s so funny. It’s like a print book is like a vinyl record. Horowitch: ( Laughs .) Rosin: ( Laughs .) Like, there will be stores that are like, We sell books and vinyl records . The bombshell of AI: One thing I noticed is that AI has an oddly formal way of speaking to you. Like, it’s a lot more like the letters you quoted from the average Civil War soldier than it is like texting. And so I know that, superficially, it seems like, Of course AI will destroy deep thinking and reading , but I also sense that AI returns a level of kind of full-sentences formality. Like, Here is how you should think through things . There is a kind of a return to discipline and what it’s presenting to you in a way that I find interesting. Horowitch: No, it’s funny. It’s sort of the ultimate irony that we talk about this technology that’s just gonna destroy thinking or whatever, and it’s text or, at least, now it communicates with us via text. And so the point that I tried to make is that we are actually living in a time where there’s more text. AI has led to a threefold increase in the number of books released on Amazon, more scientific papers being published. And a lot of this text at first brush appears polished and professional and something that you might trust and take in. And so that’s why I think it’s more important than ever to cultivate your powers of critical thinking and discernment, because AI is creating this superabundance of text, and we need to be able to sift through it. Rosin: Maybe that’s a useful way to think about this argument, that it’s not just about books. It’s not just about novels. It is about taking note of a world in which, because you are inundated with text, you can have this feeling that you are, in fact, reading a lot, but maybe pay attention to the ways in which whatever reading you’re doing is eroding an ability to think critically, think deeply, think independently. Just note that evolution or change. [ Music ] Horowitch: No, and I think that that has been true for so long. But one thing that I think we might be seeing now is that for a while at least, a lot of information on social media was presented in text. And now we sort of see with new capabilities just so much more short-form video. TikToker 1: The United States of America is becoming postliterate. TikToker 2: The end of reading is here, so I wanna talk about why this is a reality that I refuse to accept. Over the weekend, I read The Atlantic ’s August cover story, about the death of reading. TikToker 3: According to the National Endowment for the Arts, fewer than half of American adults reported having read any book in 2022. Horowitch: So I do wonder if we will get to a point where we’re not even reading more text, because it’s just videos now. TikToker 4: I’m about to talk about the death of reading on the app that is allegedly killing it. Stay with me. Rosin: Yeah, there was a really interesting interview with one of the leading figures at The New York Times who talked about the Times shifting very explicitly towards video, and that seemed like a moment. Partly just because we’re journalists, but we do think of The New York Times as a bastion of sort of literary, straight newsgathering. So it’s something interesting. And them acknowledging the future is video, we haven’t really even talked about that. We’ve been pretending as if we’re going to continue to live in a world inundated with text, but that may not be true at all. I know you interviewed a lot of professors, researchers. Did they have ideas beyond, Woe is me. I wish more people would read books ? Do they have method strategies, anything they employ that wasn’t just kind of, Do like we used to do ? Horowitch: Well, I think there are some signs that there’s a little bit of a backlash brewing. Certainly, we see it against tech in schools, you know, states banning cellphones during the school day. And when Texas did so, a district in Dallas saw 200,000 more library books checked out. So I think that— Rosin: Wait—explain the experiment again. What exactly happened? Horowitch: So Texas banned cellphones during the school day, and nearly two dozen states did it. And then the year after that happened, there were 200,000 more library books checked out at this Dallas school district than there had been the year before. So it did seem that I guess when people had more time and there was nothing else they could do, they were like, Okay, I’ll read a book . Rosin: That actually seems very simple and significant ’cause so many schools are banning cellphones during the day that it’s just out of sheer boredom. You’re like, Okay, fine, I’ll read a book. My teacher says it’s interesting. I’ll try it out. Horowitch: Right. Exactly. And so we don’t know that it inherently leads to better grades or people doing better in schools. It seems like the evidence is mixed there. But it does seem like it definitely forces people to just, Okay, we have so much more time back, so either we’re gonna hang out with each other, we’re gonna get a book, maybe do all of the above . So that’s a change. But in terms of things that we can do to kind of address this, I guess the challenge is, because I think it is such a big shift, it would require something of similar magnitude to move back to reading if that’s something that we decide is worth it and something we’re not willing to give up. And here I think it’s useful to return to how I opened the essay, which is with this metaphor about the Library of Alexandria. I wrote about that because the Library of Alexandria is known as this real intellectual center of this earlier era. And for a long time, people thought that the library was destroyed by an act of war, that Julius Caesar burned it. What the new consensus is among historians is that: No, these external attacks didn’t matter that much. The library died because people didn’t care that it lived. They neglected it, and then it eventually fell into disrepair. And so I think that is a really useful way to think about the present, that we just have to decide because we’re doing this to ourselves, that we want to choose differently. Rosin: Did doing this research change your own reading habits at all? [ Music ] Horowitch: I definitely have tried to read more. And I think what I find is just when I do have time, I always love reading and get back into it. But there’s always that little first hump that I have to get over, to kind of retrain my brain. I don’t know if that’s actually what’s happening inside my brain, but it feels like I can’t just get through it, and then I start to get engrossed, and then I stop looking at my phone as much. And so it’s definitely made me think about that much more and notice it more clearly. Rosin: I feel like that’s a no. ( Laughs .) Horowitch: ( Laughs .) I’m trying to be positive. I’m like, No, I should read more . Rosin: Shouldn’t we all. Well, Rose, thank you so much for joining me today. Horowitch: Thank you for having me. Rosin: This episode of Radio Atlantic was produced by Jinae West. It was edited by Kevin Townsend. Yvonne Kim fact-checked. Rob Smierciak engineered and provided original music. We also had music from Breakmaster Cylinder. Claudine Ebeid is the executive producer of Atlantic audio, and Andrea Valdez is our managing editor. Listeners, if you enjoy the show, you can support our work and the work of all Atlantic journalists when you subscribe to The Atlantic at TheAtlantic.com/Listener . I’m Hanna Rosin. Thank you for listening.

items.title: Plus de 140 migrants introuvables après le sauvetage d’une embarcation
items.description: Le gouvernement gambien a fait part de sa vive inquiétude après le sauvetage de 38 migrants au large de Nouadhibou, en Mauritanie. Alors qu'environ 180 personnes avaient pris place à bord de l’embarcation partie de Gambie, plus de 140 passagers demeurent portés disparus, ravivant les craintes autour de la dangereuse route migratoire de l'Atlantique. Le gouvernement de la Gambie a exprimé, mercredi 22 juillet 2026, sa profonde préoccupation à la suite du sauvetage d'une embarcation de migrants au large de Nouadhibou, dans le nord-ouest de la Mauritanie. Selon les premières informations communiquées par les autorités et relayées par des médias, le bateau avait quitté les côtes gambiennes avec environ 180 personnes à son bord, mais seuls 38 survivants ont été retrouvés à ce stade. D'après un communiqué du ministère gambien des Affaires étrangères, les rescapés ont été secourus le 18 juillet grâce à une opération menée par les garde-côtes mauritaniens, avec l'appui de pêcheurs artisanaux. Parmi eux figurent 34 hommes, deux femmes, une fille et un garçon. Les survivants ont raconté avoir dérivé pendant près de 25 jours en mer avant d'être retrouvés. Ils ont également indiqué que les passagers provenaient de plusieurs pays d'Afrique de l'Ouest, notamment de la Gambie, du Sénégal, de la Côte d'Ivoire, de la Guinée et du Mali. Les autorités mauritaniennes, en collaboration avec leurs homologues gambiennes et l'Organisation internationale pour les migrations (OIM), poursuivent les investigations afin d'identifier les survivants, de confirmer leur nationalité et de déterminer le sort des 140 personnes toujours portées disparues. Face à ce drame, le gouvernement gambien a adressé ses condoléances aux familles endeuillées et exprimé sa solidarité envers les pays dont des ressortissants pourraient compter parmi les victimes. Les autorités se sont également dites particulièrement bouleversées par le sort de deux enfants rescapés qui auraient perdu plusieurs membres de leur famille durant la traversée. À travers son ambassade à Nouakchott, Banjul affirme travailler étroitement avec les autorités mauritaniennes, l'OIM et les partenaires concernés, afin d'établir les circonstances exactes du naufrage, d'apporter une assistance consulaire aux ressortissants gambiens et de faire toute la lumière sur le destin des personnes disparues. Le gouvernement a, par ailleurs, réaffirmé son engagement à protéger ses citoyens et à renforcer la coopération régionale pour favoriser des migrations régulières et sûres, tout en intensifiant la lutte contre les réseaux de trafic de migrants et les facteurs qui alimentent les départs clandestins. Les autorités gambiennes ont également lancé un appel à leurs ressortissants, les invitant à privilégier les voies légales de migration et à éviter les traversées irrégulières, souvent synonymes de tragédies humaines. Ce nouveau drame rappelle une fois de plus la dangerosité de la route atlantique vers les îles Canaries, considérée comme l'une des plus meurtrières au monde. Selon l'OIM, au moins 1 214 migrants y ont perdu la vie ou disparu lors de 54 naufrages recensés en 2025. Quelques jours auparavant, le HCR signalait déjà la mort ou la disparition de 144 migrants au large de la Mauritanie entre le 14 et le 18 juillet, malgré le sauvetage de 387 personnes. Par Valentin SOMANDE L’article Plus de 140 migrants introuvables après le sauvetage d'une embarcation est apparu en premier sur Wakat Séra .

items.title: Marineland : huit dauphins transférés en Espagne
items.description: Des dauphins sautent lors d’un spectacle au parc à thème Marineland à Antibes, sur la Côte d’Azur (sud-est de la France), le 17 mars 2016. Les orques et les dauphins du Marineland d’Antibes (Alpes-Maritimes) ne peuvent pas être transférés en Espagne, suite à un avis défavorable de l’autorité scientifique espagnole, et doivent y rester pour le moment, a indiqué le cabinet du ministre français de la Transition écologique à l’AFP le 10 avril 2025. (Photo de VALERY HACHE / AFP) VALERY HACHE / AFP
